Use Case Guide

Construction Software for Project Tracking (2026)

You can't manage what you can't see. Here's the software that gives you real visibility into your active projects.

✓ Updated April 2026

Project tracking is the difference between knowing you have a problem and being surprised by it. The contractors who stay profitable long-term have systems that tell them where every job stands — budget, schedule, outstanding issues — without waiting for the job to close out.

Here's the best software for the specific use case of tracking your projects in real time.

What real project tracking requires

Not all "project management" software actually gives you project tracking. Real tracking means:

  • Budget vs. actual costs updated in near-real-time (not once a month from accounting)
  • Schedule progress tied to actual completion — not just planned dates
  • Committed costs — purchase orders and approved change orders that will become real costs
  • Outstanding items — open RFIs, submittals, punch list items that affect completion
  • Cross-project dashboard — visibility across all active jobs, not just one at a time

Best platforms for construction project tracking

Buildertrend — Best for residential builders

Price: $499–$1,099/mo

Buildertrend's project tracking is built around two views: the budget dashboard and the Gantt schedule. The budget dashboard shows your original estimate, approved change orders, committed costs (POs), actual costs (bills entered or synced from QuickBooks), and the resulting over/under by cost category. The Gantt schedule shows which tasks are complete, which are in progress, and which are late — with automatic cascading when delays occur.

The cross-project view is where Buildertrend shines for multi-project residential builders — you can see which projects are on budget and which are running hot across your entire portfolio from one screen.

Buildertrend — Real-time budget and schedule tracking built for residential construction. Request a demo to see the tracking dashboard.

Visit Buildertrend →

JobTread — Best for small GCs who prioritize financial tracking

Price: $99–$499/mo

JobTread's job costing is consistently praised by smaller contractors who found Buildertrend's price unjustifiable. Real-time revenue vs. cost vs. projected profit per job, with the ability to drill into cost categories to see where overruns are happening. The dashboard gives a clear picture of profitability across all active jobs.

Procore — Best for commercial GC tracking

Price: Custom

Procore's project tracking spans financial (budget, cost to complete projections, change order status, payment status) and operational (RFI cycle times, submittal status, punch list completion). The executive dashboard rolls up portfolio-level data — useful for owners and principals who want a summary view across all active projects. Cost-to-complete forecasting is particularly valuable for commercial GCs managing complex multi-phase projects.

Houzz Pro — For tracking client-facing project progress

Price: $65–$399/mo

Houzz Pro is lighter on pure financial tracking but strong on client-facing project progress: selection status, project timeline milestones, and communication history. For remodeling contractors where the primary tracking challenge is managing client expectations and selections, not financial variance, Houzz Pro works well.

Houzz Pro — Project timeline and progress tracking with client-facing visibility.

Visit Houzz Pro →

Key tracking metrics to monitor weekly

Metric What It Tells You Action Trigger
Budget variance by category Where you're over or under budget Any category over 5% warrants review
Committed cost % Total contracted spend as % of budget Over 90% with work remaining = risk
Schedule completion % Tasks complete vs. planned by date Under 80% of planned = schedule risk
Open change orders Changes not yet approved or priced More than 2 weeks outstanding = follow up
Outstanding invoices Billings not yet paid by client Over 30 days = collections issue

Why real-time tracking changes the business

Most contractors who've adopted proper project tracking software describe a fundamental shift: they stop being surprised. Instead of discovering a job lost money at closeout — when there's nothing left to do — they see the overrun developing at 40% completion, when they still have leverage to negotiate additional scope, reduce overhead, or recover schedule.

The contractors who consistently stay profitable aren't smarter than the ones who don't. They have better visibility, earlier. Software makes that visibility systematic rather than dependent on a single person staying on top of everything manually.

Frequently asked questions

How often should I review project tracking data?

Weekly at minimum for active projects. The goal of real-time tracking isn't to obsess over numbers daily — it's to establish a regular review cadence where you catch problems while they're still manageable. Most experienced contractors recommend a 15–30 minute weekly review of budget vs. actual for every active job, with deeper dives when variance exceeds a threshold.

Can tracking software replace a project manager?

No — software provides the data, but humans need to interpret it and act on it. Tracking software makes a good project manager more effective and makes smaller operations able to operate with less dedicated PM overhead. It doesn't replace judgment.
